elements.sent-2411-001: Achieving high levels of safety and reliability in autonomous driving remains a critical challenge, especially due to occlusion and limited perception ranges in stand-alone systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Cooperative perception among vehicles offers a promising solution, but existing research is hindered by datasets with a limited number of agents.
elements.sent-2411-003: Scaling up the number of cooperating agents is non-trivial and introduces significant computational and technical hurdles that have not been addressed in previous works.
elements.sent-2411-004: To bridge this gap, we present Wireless enHanced Autonomous vehicles with Large number of Engaged agentS (WHALES), a dataset generated using CARLA simulator that features an unprecedented average of 8.4 agents per driving sequence.
elements.sent-2411-005: In addition to providing the largest number of agents and viewpoints among autonomous driving datasets, WHALES records agent behaviors, enabling cooperation across multiple tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: This expansion allows for new supporting tasks in cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-007: As a demonstration, we conduct experiments on agent scheduling task, where the ego agent selects one of multiple candidate agents to cooperate with, optimizing perception gains in autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-008: The WHALES dataset and codebase can be found at \href{github {https://github.com/chensiweiTHU/WHALES .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Autonomous Driving (AD) technology holds the promise of preventing traffic accidents, enhancing mobility for disabled individuals, and improving overall traffic safety and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-010: Recent advancements in deep learning have brought significant breakthroughs to AD technologies in various perception tasks such as 3D object detection\cite{liu2023bevfusion , 4D occupancy prediction\cite{tian2024occ3d , multi-object tracking\cite{meinhardt2022trackformer , and end-to-end planning\cite{planning .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: To navigate through complex surrounding environment, AD systems utilize different sensor arrays, such as cameras, LiDARs, radars and IMU, to perceive their surroundings and make impactful decisions on ego-vehicle behavior.
elements.sent-2411-012: Howeve, stand-alone or single-vehicle AD systems face a critical limitation in perception: their inability to detect information from Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS) regions, leading to significant occlusions that pose potential safety risk.
elements.sent-2411-013: To overcome this, researchers have introduced cooperative driving through wireless communication, which enhances safety, particularly in complex and challenging road scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-014: The NR-V2X technology outlined by 3GPP in Releases 17 and 18\cite{NR supports raw, feature and object-level fusion in cooperative driving, enabling cooperation under groupcast and unicast modes.}
elements.sent-2411-015: These cooperative driving strategies, which enables collaboration between connected autonomous vehicles (CAV) and roadside units (RSU), pave the way for safer and more efficient autonomous transportation systems.

elements.sent-2411-016: Several studies \cite{xu2022v2xvit,hu2022where2comm,lei2022syncnet,li2023lossy,hu2023coca have focused on enhancing the robustness of cooperative perception (CP) by addressing challenges posed by non-ideal factors in the fields of autonomous driving and communication, such as communication latency, sensor localization errors, and various communication constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In these studies, intelligent agents improve perception at different stages of the model: raw sensory data is fused in early fusion, processed encoded features are combined in intermediate fusion and detection results are integrated in late fusion\cite{han2023collaborative , which leads to improved average precision\cite{xu2022v2xvit}\cite{hu2022where2comm}\cite{hu2023coca .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Other research efforts\cite{lei2022syncnet}\cite{li2023lossy}\cite{lu2023robust aim to enhance cooperative perception by modeling the non-ideal factors that arise when autonomous driving systems are combined with communication, ensuring that the proposed models remain robust under these conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-019: To promote innovative research in cooperative autonomous driving, we introduce a multi-agent scheduling dataset, namely Wireless enHanced Autonomous vehicles with Large number of Engaged agentS (WHALES), which covers 70K RGB images, 17K LiDAR frames and 2.01M 3D bounding box annotations.
elements.sent-2411-020: As shown in Fig. \ref{figure1 , WHALES features various road scenarios, including intersections, T-junctions, highway ramps, roundabout, and 5-way intersections.}
elements.sent-2411-021: It goes beyond conventional perception-based approaches to enable more comprehensive cooperative functionalities.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our dataset and benchmarking tasks are specifically designed to foster innovation and drive progress in V2X cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-023: Fig. \ref{figure2 provides an overview of a single frame from the WHALES dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In Fig. \ref{figure2:subfig:a , the bird's-eye view (BEV) of the scenario is shown.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Fig. \ref{figure2:subfig:b visualizes the point clouds from all agents, including one RSU, within the frame, while the Fig. \ref{figure2:subfig:c shows the front camera views of all agents.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The ego vehicle can detect surrounding objects by properly scheduling cooperation with nearby agents.
elements.sent-2411-027: Experiments conducted on the WHALES dataset cover stand-alone and cooperative 3D perception, as well as agent scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-028: Agent scheduling, a new task introduced by our dataset, extends beyond what has been explored in previous research.
elements.sent-2411-029: We implement the dataset using MMDetection3D\cite{MMDetection3D and provide baseline models along with corresponding performance metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Existing agent scheduling methods are incorporated into preprocessing pipelines, enabling researchers to easily propose and evaluate new scheduling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-032: We build WHALES, a large-scale scheduling dataset supporting V2V and V2I perception.
elements.sent-2411-033: By significantly optimizing the simulation speed and computation cost of CARLA when handling a large number of agents, all the scenes are generated with multi-modal sensors and features an average of 8.4 agents per driving sequence.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our dataset includes more than 2.01M annotated 3D bounding boxes, along with object indices and agent behavior information, allowing for strong extensibility to downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-035: We illustrate the applications of our dataset on two cooperative tasks, including 3D object detection and agent scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-036: To our knowledge, this is the first dataset to consider scheduling in cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-037: We provide detailed benchmarks for both tasks, and for agent scheduling, we provide analysis on various existing single-agent and multi-agent scheduling algorithms.

elements.sent-2411-038: Large open datasets play an essential role to advancing collaborative perception in autonomous driving systems.
elements.sent-2411-039: Earlier works, including KITTI \cite{kitti , nuScenes\cite{nuscenes and Waymo Open\cite{waymo , provide real-world scenarios supporting tasks like 3D object detection and tracking.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: For motion forecasting and planning, Argoverse\cite{argoverse and Argoverse2\cite{argoverse2 datasets offer valuable data, including tracking and motion forecasting annotations, and semantic metadata.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Additionally, the nuPlan\cite{nuplan dataset introduces a closed-loop, machine-learning-based planning benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, these datasets are limited to single-vehicle scenarios, restricting their applicability for collaborative perception due to the limited perceptual fields of individual vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-043: To address this limitation, Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) perception has emerged as a solution.
elements.sent-2411-044: By enabling information exchange between vehicles or with roadside units, V2X perception significantly enhances the capability of autonomous vehicles to handle challenging conditions such as occlusions and long-range perception.
elements.sent-2411-045: In the realm of real-world datasets, DAIR-V2X\cite{dairv2x stands out as the first large-scale dataset dedicated to collaborative perception for 3D object detection.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, V2V4Real\cite{v2v4real offers data from two vehicles equipped with LiDARs and cameras, facilitating 3D object detection and tracking.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The V2X-Seq\cite{v2xseq dataset expands the resource pool by providing comprehensive sequential data frames, trajectories, vector maps, and traffic light information from real-world settings.}
elements.sent-2411-048: While numerous real-world datasets have emerged, the scale of datasets specifically designed for cooperative perception remains constrained for various reasons.
elements.sent-2411-049: For instance, datasets like DAIR-V2X\cite{dairv2x and V2X-Seq\cite{v2xseq are geographically limited to Beijing, China.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Due to the challenges of collecting real-world data, simulation-based approaches have gained popularity in generating collaborative perception datasets.
elements.sent-2411-051: OPV2V\cite{opv2v is a significant development in this area, being the first large-scale simulated dataset for Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) perception.}
elements.sent-2411-052: It encompasses diverse environments, including eight towns within the CARLA\cite{carla simulator and a digital representation of Culver City.}
elements.sent-2411-053: V2X-Sim\cite{v2xsim extends this by supporting both V2V and Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) perception.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Additionally, DOLPHINS\cite{dolphins contributes to the field by enabling V2V and V2I perceptions, introducing a broader range of scenarios with dynamic weather conditions.}

elements.sent-2411-058: For instance, DOLPHINS\cite{dolphins and OPV2V\cite{opv2v contain an average of three viewpoints per scene, supporting V2V perception.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Most existing datasets are primarily designed for perception tasks, with limited scene lengths, and they rely solely on CARLA auto-pilot system for planning and control.
elements.sent-2411-060: Most existing datasets assume ideal conditions for cooperative perception.

elements.sent-2411-063: Table \ref{table1 highlights the strengths of our dataset over existing cooperative perception datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-064: WHALES offers several unique advantages, including the extensive support for V2V and V2I, and rich road information through a large number of viewpoints per scene.
elements.sent-2411-065: With an average of 8.4 viewpoints per scene, our dataset surpasses others in its capability to evaluate the performance of different multi-agent scheduling algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-066: WHALES contains 17K LiDAR point clouds and 70K images, and its scenes exhibit a higher density of agents compared to other datasets.
elements.sent-2411-067: We defined a valid 3D bounding box as one that is within 50 meters of an agent's view.
elements.sent-2411-068: By this definition, there are 2.01M valid 3D bounding boxes across multiple views.
elements.sent-2411-069: There are a total of 511K unique 3D bounding boxes across frames in the dataset, exceeding the number present in other existing datasets.
elements.sent-2411-070: As the number of agents increases in a confined area, the simulation time and computational cost grows non-linearly due to the increase in agent-wise interactions.
elements.sent-2411-071: To address this challenge, we assign a separate process for each active agent to generate raw data, storing the results in a queue.
elements.sent-2411-072: We ensure these agent processes are synchronized, and their observations are collated into batches.
elements.sent-2411-073: An expert model is then used to generate the agent behaviors simultaneously from the batch observations.
elements.sent-2411-074: On average, our simulator requires approximately 160ms per additional agent on an NVIDIA 4090 GPU, effectively reducing the time cost to a linear function, making the time and computational cost acceptable for dataset generation.
elements.sent-2411-075: Table \ref{table:sensor-specs and \ref{agent_config outlines the sensor and agent configurations in the WHALES dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The dataset provides multi-modal sensory input for the intelligent agents, balancing complexity with the tasks supported by the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-077: In total, we define four types of agent.
elements.sent-2411-078: The first type of agent is the uncontrolled CAVs.
elements.sent-2411-079: The second type of agent is the controlled CAVs.
elements.sent-2411-080: Both types of CAVs are equipped with one 64-beam LiDAR and four 1920$\times$1080 cameras, alongside with V2X communication devices.
elements.sent-2411-081: With the setup, they can perform perception and communication tasks.
elements.sent-2411-082: Controlled vehicles can be controlled through self-defined planning and control algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-083: The third type is the RSU installed on the roadside, also equipped with LiDARs and cameras.
elements.sent-2411-084: The fourth type is the non-player or obstacle agents, which do not have any sensors attached and have uncontrolled trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-085: Under this setup, we are able to evaluate the enhancement of safety and other benchmarks of AD contributed by cooperation.
elements.sent-2411-086: Our dataset is organized into scenes, frames, samples and annotations.
elements.sent-2411-087: A scene represents a segment of the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-088: In the simulation, we set the sampling interval to 0.1 seconds, while one frame is saved every 0.5s.
elements.sent-2411-089: For every scene, we provide the basic simulation configuration information and two videos: one shows the front camera views of all agents, while the other presents an encoded BEV map similar to that in\cite{roach .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Within every frame, the object class, location, rotation and velocity of every agent are recorded along with annotations in the world coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-091: In one frame, each agents plays the role of ego vehicle in turn, thus we have $M$ samples per frame in a scenario with $M$ intelligent agents.
elements.sent-2411-092: We project bounding boxes into ego vehicles' coordinate systems and filter valid boxes within perception range to generate annotations in one sample.
elements.sent-2411-093: We generate the dataset using a modified version CARLA Simulator\cite{carla .}
elements.sent-2411-094: Additionally, we inherit a reinforcement learning environment from\cite{roach , which includes some of the fundamental world settings from\cite{opencda .}}
elements.sent-2411-095: To create an environment conducive to cooperation, all agents are spawned within a rectangular area surrounding a central point.
elements.sent-2411-096: Our experimental configuration system is integrated in MMDetection3D\cite{MMDetection3D , which utilizes a Python file to store experimental settings including model structure, data processing pipelines and agent scheduling policies.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution display the orientations of the bounding box annotations in our dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-098: As shown in Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution:subfig:a , the bounding boxes surrounding the ego vehicle are distributed across various orientations.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Due to the constraints of road orientation, many objects are aligned either parallel or perpendicular to the ego vehicle's direction, showing a concentrated shade of blue in the four directions.
elements.sent-2411-100: Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution:subfig:b shows the distribution of the distance between cooperative agents.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Most vehicles are positioned close to one another, providing complementary viewpoints, while some are placed farther apart to offer long-range information.
elements.sent-2411-102: Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution:subfig:c illustrates the distribution of the minimum distances between agents and objects, with the closest objects typically within 20 meters of the agent.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Our annotation scheme follows the conventions of the nuScenes dataset\cite{nuscenes .}
elements.sent-2411-104: It is important to note that all annotations are generated using the CARLA simulator\cite{carla .}
elements.sent-2411-105: The model is expected to detect all objects within the valid detection range of 50 meters, including occluded objects.
elements.sent-2411-106: In addition to existing nuScenes labels, new annotations for cooperative perception tasks are included, such as graphs depicting the distance and occlusion relationships between agents.

elements.sent-2411-170: We present WHALES, a multi-agent scheduling dataset containing 17K LiDAR frames, 70K RGB images, and a total of 2.01M 3D annotations, to advance research in V2V cooperative autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-171: We enhance CARLA's simulation spped by synchronizing agent processes for raw data generation batching data processing through a deep learning model.
elements.sent-2411-172: Additionally, we introduce benchmarks for 3D object detection and agent scheduling to showcase the strengths of our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-173: We implement various scheduling algorithms alongside our 3D object detection to evaluate the impact of scheduling on cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-174: Our dataset also features controllable agents behaviors, enabling research into agent interaction and control strategies.
elements.sent-2411-175: In the future, we aim to expand cooperation in autonomous driving beyond perception by implementing modular and end-to-end autonomous driving systems using this dataset and evaluating the safety improvements contributed by cooperation.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Cooperative perception research is constrained by the scarcity of datasets that capture the complexity of real-world Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) interactions, particularly under dynamic communication constraints.
elements.sent-2411-177: To address this, we present WHALES (Wireless enHanced Autonomous vehicles with Large number of Engaged agentS), the first large-scale V2X dataset specifically designed to benchmark communication-aware agent scheduling and scalable cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-178: WHALES establishes a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) standard with an average of 8.4 cooperative agents per scene, and 2.01 million annotated 3D objects spanning diverse traffic scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-179: It integrates communication metadata to simulate real-world communication bottlenecks, enabling rigorous evaluation of scheduling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-180: To further advance the field, we propose the Coverage-Aware Historical Scheduler (CAHS), a novel scheduling baseline that prioritizes agents based on historical viewpoint coverage, improving perception performance over existing SOTA methods.
elements.sent-2411-181: WHALES bridges the gap between simulated and real-world V2X challenges, offering a robust framework to explore perception-scheduling co-design, cross-data generalization, and scalability limits.
elements.sent-2411-182: The WHALES dataset and code are available at \href{github {https://github.com/chensiweiTHU/WHALES .}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Modern autonomous vehicles rely on multi-modal sensor arrays: cameras, LiDARs, radars and IMUs to perceive complex environments and inform real-time decisions.
elements.sent-2411-184: Despite remarkable progress in perception tasks such as 3D object detection\cite{liu2023bevfusion , 4D occupancy prediction\cite{tian2024occ3d , multi-object tracking\cite{meinhardt2022trackformer , and end-to-end planning\cite{planning , isolated perception systems remain inherently limited by sensor range, occlusions, and computational latency.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Cooperative perception, which enables vehicles and infrastructure to share sensor data via Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) networks, addresses these limitations by aggregating multi-agent perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-186: However, the development of robust cooperative algorithms is hindered by the absence of datasets that reflect real-world V2X challenges: scalability, communication constraints, and dynamic interactions.

elements.sent-2411-188: However, existing datasets fall short in critical dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-189: Current benchmarks such as DAIR-V2X\cite{dairv2x and DOLPHINS\cite{dolphins are constrained to small-scale scenarios with 2-3 agents per scene, failing to replicate real-world V2X environments where dynamic interactions among dozens of vehicles and infrastructure nodes are commonplace.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: These datasets also lack communication metadata which is essential for evaluating robustness against non-ideal factors explored in prior works.
elements.sent-2411-191: While simulated datasets like OPV2V\cite{opv2v support Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) cooperation, they omit infrastructure agents (V2I) and adversarial conditions such as non-line-of-sight (NLOS) occlusions.}
elements.sent-2411-192: Furthermore, their static scenarios and fixed agent roles prevent research into adaptive scheduling or scalability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-193: These gaps limit progress toward communication-efficient, large-scale cooperative perception systems.
elements.sent-2411-194: To overcome these barriers, we present WHALES, or Wireless enHanced Autonomous vehicles with Large number of Engaged agentS, a comprehensive V2X dataset featuring 70K RGB images, 17K LiDAR frames and 2.01M 3D bounding box annotations.
elements.sent-2411-195: As shown in Fig. \ref{figure1 , WHALES models diverse road scenarios, including intersections, T-junctions, highway ramps, roundabouts, and 5-way intersections.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Unlike prior autonomous driving datasets, WHALES integrates dynamic communication constraints, heterogeneous agents, including vehicles and infrastructure, and adversarial NLOS conditions, enabling detailed study of agent scheduling, model scalability and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-197: Our benchmarks extend beyond conventional perception tasks by including agent scheduling and communication-aware fusion, promoting innovation in adaptive V2X systems.

elements.sent-2411-200: We present the first simulated dataset enabling research on communication-constrained agent scheduling in V2X perception, featuring dynamic multi-agent scenarios, including adversarial NLOS conditions and occlusion patterns that require cooperative reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-201: We propose the Coverage-Aware Historical Scheduler (CAHS) algorithm, a novel scheduling method that prioritizes agents based on their historical viewpoint coverage within the ego vehicle’s perception range.
elements.sent-2411-202: Through a handshake mechanism, CAHS dynamically selects candidates with maximal spatial relevance.
elements.sent-2411-203: We establish the benchmarks for 3D object detection and agent scheduling under communication constraints, and provide detailed analysis on one proposed and various existing baseline single-agent and multi-agent scheduling algorithms.

elements.sent-2411-222: WHALES offers unique advantages, providing comprehensive support for both V2V and V2I interactions, and detailed road context through dense multi-agent scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-223: Our benchmark enables thorough evaluation of multi-agent scheduling algorithms under realistic conditions.
elements.sent-2411-224: A valid 3D bounding box is defined for any object within a 50-meter radius of an agent's viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-225: The computational cost of simulating dense multi-agent interactions grows non-linearly with agent density due to complex inter-agent dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-226: To address this, a parallelized pipeline is implemented where each active agent operates in dedicated process, streaming raw sensor data to a synchronized queue.
elements.sent-2411-227: These processes are dynamically synchronized, and agents' observations are aggregated into batches for efficient processing.
elements.sent-2411-228: A centralized expert model then generates agent behaviors from batched observations.
elements.sent-2411-229: This architecture reduces simulation time to approximately 160ms per additional agent on an NVIDIA 4090 GPU, transforming time complexity into a linear function and making large-scale dataset generation computationally feasible.
elements.sent-2411-230: The sensor and agent configurations in the WHALES dataset are detailed in Tables \ref{table:sensor-specs and \ref{agent_config .}}
elements.sent-2411-231: The dataset provides multi-modal sensory input for the intelligent agents, balancing realism with computational tractability.
elements.sent-2411-232: Four agent types are defined: (1) Uncontrolled CAVs, which follow predefined trajectories without user input; (2) Controlled CAVs, which are functionally identical but can be controlled via custom planning and control algorithms; (3) Roadside Units (RSUs), which are infrastructure agents equipped with infrastructure-grade LiDARs and cameras; and (4) Non-Player (Obstacle) Agents, which lack sensors and follow uncontrolled trajectories to simulate ambient traffic.
elements.sent-2411-233: All CAVs and RSUs are equipped with a 64-beam LiDAR, four 1920 $\times$ 1080 RGB cameras, and V2X communication modules.
elements.sent-2411-234: Our dataset is hierarchically organized into scenes, frames, samples and annotations.
elements.sent-2411-235: A scene represents a continuous segment of the simulation, configured with specific environmental conditions and agent interactions.
elements.sent-2411-236: During simulation, data is sampled at intervals of 0.1 seconds, while full frames are recorded every 0.5 seconds to balance temporal resolution and storage efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-237: Each scene includes comprehensive simulation metadata and two synchronized video streams: one capturing the front camera perspectives of all agents, and another providing an encoded BEV map for holistic scene visualization, inspired by the methodology in \cite{roach .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Within every frame, object class, 3D location, rotation, velocity, and unique track identifiers are recorded for all agents in a global coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-239: To support cooperative perception tasks, every agent alternately assumes the role of the ego vehicle within the same frame.
elements.sent-2411-240: This design generates $N$ samples per frame in scenarios with $N$ agents, ensuring diverse ego-centric perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-241: Annotations are generated by projecting bounding boxes into each ego vehicle's local coordinate system and filtering objects within the perception range, reflecting real-world sensor limitations.
elements.sent-2411-242: The dataset is generated using a modified version of the CARLA simulator\cite{carla , augmented with a reinforcement learning (RL) framework inherited from\cite{roach and foundational world configurations from\cite{opencda .}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: To simulate realistic cooperative scenarios, agents are dynamically spawned within a rectangular region centered around critical traffic points.
elements.sent-2411-244: This approach ensures dense multi-agent interactions while maintaining scene plausibility.
elements.sent-2411-245: Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution displays the spatial characteristics of annotated bounding boxes in our dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-246: As shown in Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution:subfig:a , bounding boxes exhibit diverse orientations relative to the ego vehicle.}
elements.sent-2411-247: The distribution reveals strong alignment with road topology, as a majority of objects are oriented parallel or perpendicular to the ego vehicle's direction, with density peaks at 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270°.
elements.sent-2411-248: Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution:subfig:b illustrates the spatial distribution of cooperative agents.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Most agents are clustered within 50 meters of one another, enabling complementary viewpoint coverage, while a subset is positioned beyond 100 meters to simulate long-range perception scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-250: Fig. \ref{dataset_distribution:subfig:c quantifies object proximity, showing that 88\% of objects lie within 20 meters of the nearest agent, emphasizing the prevalence of occlusions in dense urban settings.}
elements.sent-2411-251: Our annotation protocol adheres to the nuScenes benchmark standards\cite{nuscenes , with all labels generated using the CARLA ground-truth output \cite{carla .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: Annotations include all objects within a 50-meter valid detection range, including occluded instances, to reflect real-world perceptual challenges.
elements.sent-2411-253: Beyond standard 3D bounding boxes, we introduce cooperative-specific metadata such as inter-agent occlusion matrices and distance graphs to facilitate scheduling algorithm development.

elements.sent-2411-304: We introduce WHALES, the first V2X dataset designed to address scalability and communication-aware scheduling in cooperative perception, addresses the limitations in existing datasets by simulating dynamic multi-agent interactions under real-world communication constraints.
elements.sent-2411-305: To further explore agent scheduling in cooperative perception, we propose the Coverage-Aware Historical Scheduler (CAHS), a novel scheduling algorithm that optimizes the trade-off between viewpoint diversity and communication efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-306: Future Work: We aim to extend WHALES to support joint optimization of perception, scheduling, and trajectory planning in modular and end-to-end autonomous systems.
elements.sent-2411-307: To generate more realistic scenarios, we will integrate V2X channel simulators to study the impacts of packet loss and transmission latency on cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-308: These extensions will build on CAHS's framework to advance robust, communication-efficient cooperative perception in safety-critical scenarios.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
elements.sent-2411-309: Cooperative perception research is hindered by the limited availability of datasets that capture the complexity of real-world Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) interactions, particularly under dynamic communication constraints.
elements.sent-2411-310: To address this gap, we introduce WHALES (Wireless enHanced Autonomous vehicles with Large number of Engaged agentS), the first large-scale V2X dataset explicitly designed to benchmark communication-aware agent scheduling and scalable cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-311: WHALES introduces a new benchmark that enables state-of-the-art (SOTA) research in communication-aware cooperative perception, featuring an average of 8.4 cooperative agents per scene and 2.01 million annotated 3D objects across diverse traffic scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-312: It incorporates detailed communication metadata to emulate real-world communication bottlenecks, enabling rigorous evaluation of scheduling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-313: To further advance the field, we propose the Coverage-Aware Historical Scheduler (CAHS), a novel scheduling baseline that selects agents based on historical viewpoint coverage, improving perception performance over existing SOTA methods.
elements.sent-2411-314: WHALES bridges the gap between simulated and real-world V2X challenges, providing a robust framework for exploring perception-scheduling co-design, cross-data generalization, and scalability limits.
elements.sent-2411-315: Modern autonomous vehicles rely on multi-modal sensor arrays to perceive complex environments and inform real-time decisions \cite{yurtsever2020survey .}
elements.sent-2411-316: Despite significant advances in perception tasks such as 3D object detection\cite{liu2023bevfusion , 4D occupancy prediction\cite{tian2024occ3d , multi-object tracking\cite{meinhardt2022trackformer , and end-to-end planning\cite{planning , isolated perception systems remain fundamentally constrained by limited sensor range, occlusions, and latency.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-317: Cooperative perception addresses these challenges by enabling data sharing between agents via Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) networks, aggregating multi-agent perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-318: However, the development of robust cooperative algorithms is hindered by the lack of datasets that capture the complexity of real-world V2X environments.

elements.sent-2411-356: The computational cost of simulating dense multi-agent interactions increases non-linearly with agent density due to complex inter-agent dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-357: To address this, we implement a parallelized pipeline in which each active agent operates in a dedicated process, streaming raw sensor data to a synchronized queue.

elements.sent-2411-363: Four agent types are defined: (1) Uncontrolled CAVs, which follow predefined trajectories without external input; (2) Controlled CAVs, which are similarly equipped but controlled via user-defined planning algorithms; (3) Roadside Units (RSUs), infrastructure agents outfitted with high-grade LiDARs and cameras; and (4) Obstacle Agents, which lack sensors and follow autonomous trajectories to represent ambient traffic participants.
elements.sent-2411-364: The dataset is hierarchically organized into scenes, frames, samples, and annotations.
elements.sent-2411-365: A scene represents a continuous simulation segment defined by specific environmental conditions and agent interactions.
elements.sent-2411-366: During simulation, sensor data is sampled at 0.1-second intervals, while full frames are recorded every 0.5 seconds to balance temporal resolution and storage efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-367: Each scene includes comprehensive simulation metadata and two synchronized video streams: one capturing the front-camera perspectives of all agents, and the other providing an encoded BEV map for holistic visualization, following the methodology of \cite{roach .}
elements.sent-2411-368: In every frame, the object class, 3D location, rotation, velocity, and unique track ID are recorded for all agents using a shared global coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-369: To fully support cooperative perception tasks and ego-centric learning, WHALES cyclically assigns each agent the role of the ego vehicle within every frame.
elements.sent-2411-370: This design choice yields $N$ distinct samples per frame in scenarios with $N$ agents, capturing diverse ego-centric viewpoints.
elements.sent-2411-371: Annotations are generated by projecting global bounding boxes into each ego vehicle's local coordinate frame and filtering objects based on the perceptual range to better reflect real-world sensor limitations.
elements.sent-2411-372: The dataset is generated using a modified version of the CARLA simulator\cite{carla , integrated with a reinforcement learning (RL) framework from\cite{roach and base environment configurations from\cite{opencda .}}}
elements.sent-2411-373: To simulate realistic cooperative scenarios, agents are dynamically spawned within rectangular regions centered on key traffic zones.

(2) Benchmark Calculation: Each candidate calculates scheduling benchmark relative to the ego's perception range. (3) Benchmark Feedback: Candidates send their benchmark scores back to the ego. elements.sent-2411-429: These findings underscore the importance of dynamic agent scheduling to select a subset of spatially diverse and complementary agents, rather than aggregating data from all agents indiscriminately.
elements.sent-2411-430: We introduce WHALES, the first V2X dataset specifically designed to address scalability and communication-aware scheduling in cooperative perception.
elements.sent-2411-431: WHALES fills critical gaps in existing benchmarks by enabling scheduling evaluation under redundant agent coverage, realistic communication budgets, and scalable perception across densely populated scenes, capabilities that existing datasets do not jointly provide.
elements.sent-2411-432: To further advance agent scheduling, we propose the Coverage-Aware Historical Scheduler (CAHS), a novel algorithm that balances viewpoint diversity and communication efficiency to improve perception performance.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
